coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon dots-copper nanoclusters ratiometric photoluminescent nano-composites for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zili
Pang, Shujie
Wang, Mao
Wu, Miao
Li, Pengjun
Bai, Jinna
Yang, Xudong
coredata.dc:description: A novel dual-emission photoluminescent (PL) nano-materials of carbon dots-copper nanoclusters (CDs-CuNCs) nano-composites is prepared for excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+. The nano-composites are composed of blue photoluminescent CDs and red photoluminescent CuNCs with similar excitation wavelengths through the electrostatic assembly. The red photoluminescence of CuNCs was inhibited by the nano-composites exposed to Hg2+, while the blue photoluminescence of CDs remained stable. The color of the nano-composites slowly changed from pink to blue with the added of Hg2+ concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) of the nano-composites is 0.31 nmol/L (nM) toward Hg2+ in aqueous solution, when the signal to noise ratio is 3. In addition, a visual PL test paper is prepared. When the Hg2+ solution is added, the color of test paper transforms from pink to blue immediately. Therefore, the nano-composites are very important for efficient and sensitive detection of Hg2+, which show broad application prospects in environmental analysis, food safety detection, biological detection and medical diagnosis in daily life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Copper nanoclusters
Nano-composites
Dual-emission
Photoluminescence
Hg2+ detection

Hg2+ detection experiment using CDs-CuNCs nano-composites as probe
Preparation of the PL test paper
Characteristics of the CDs, CuNCs and CDs-CuNCs nano-composites
Sensitivity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites for Hg2+
Reproducibility, high stability and specificity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites
Visual detection for Hg2+ using the PL test paper

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CDs, (b) CuNCs, and (c) CDs-CuNCs nano-composites; (d) The EDS spectrum of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites.
(a) XPS spectra of CDs and CuNCs; XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) N 1s and (e) S 2p spectra for the CDs; (f) Cu 2p spectrum for CuNCs.

UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of (a) CDs and (c) CuNCs, inset: the photos of CDs and CuNCs under sunlight and UV lamp; The emission spectra of (b) CDs and (d) CuNCs with various excitation wavelengths.
Emission spectra for CDs, CuNCs and CDs-CuNCs nano-composites, inset: the photos of CDs, CuNCs and CDs-CuNCs nano-composites under sunlight and UV lamp.

(a) Ratiometric PL spectra (Ex = 345 nm) of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites versus the concentration of Hg2+(0–40,000 nM). (b) PL intensity ratio (I430/I647) of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites versus the concentration of Hg2+. (c) CIE chromaticity diagram of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites. (d) Digital photographs of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites with different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–40,000 nM) by sunlight and UV light illumination.
The PL intensity ratio (I430/I647) of CDs-CuNCs nanocomposites when Hg2+ concentration was 10 nM and common interfering ion concentration was 50 nM.
The photos of test paper by the different concentration of Hg2+ (0–40,000 nM) under visible light (a) and under a 365 nm ultraviolet light (b).
Schematic illustration of Hg2+ by CDs-CuNCs nano-composites.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon dots-copper nanoclusters ratiometric photoluminescent nano-composites for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun, 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel dual-emission photoluminescent (PL) nano-materials of carbon dots-copper nanoclusters (CDs-CuNCs) nano-composites is prepared for excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward Hg2+. The nano-composites are composed of blue photoluminescent CDs and red photoluminescent CuNCs with similar excitation wavelengths through the electrostatic assembly. The red photoluminescence of CuNCs was inhibited by the nano-composites exposed to Hg2+, while the blue photoluminescence of CDs remained stable. The color of the nano-composites slowly changed from pink to blue with the added of Hg2+ concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) of the nano-composites is 0.31 nmol/L (nM) toward Hg2+ in aqueous solution, when the signal to noise ratio is 3. In addition, a visual PL test paper is prepared. When the Hg2+ solution is added, the color of test paper transforms from pink to blue immediately. Therefore, the nano-composites are very important for efficient and sensitive detection of Hg2+, which show broad application prospects in environmental analysis, food safety detection, biological detection and medical diagnosis in daily life.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+), as an important and familiar heavy metal ion, is considered as a hazardous pollutant in the world [1–3]. Hg2+ pollution caused by human daily life has always been an environmental problem, which is also because of its extensive emissions and high toxicity. On the other hand, Hg2+ can be easily absorbed and accumulated by the human body because of their non-degradability and bioaccumulation [4–6]. This may lead to all kinds of damage to the kidneys, heart and brain, and so far as to irreversible injury to the nervous system in the body [7–9]. Recently, there are many methods and techniques have been used for Hg2+ detection, including X-ray absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry [10,11], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and atomic absorption spectroscopy [11–13]. However, majority of these approaches have some shortcoming such as complex sample preparation, time-consuming, require expensive apparatus and laborious detection process. To date, many optical signal sensors were reported owing to their advantages of easy design, simple handle and good stability [14,15]. For example, inorganic quantum dots, gold and silver nanoparticles, electrochemical sensors and organic dyes are often used as photoluminescent or colorimetric probes in practical application and show the good selective, simple operation and economy.

Carbon dots are a newfashioned type of carbon-based zero-dimensional photoluminescent materials emerging in the past decade. Most of them are spherical or subglobular particles with diameter not exceeding 10 nm. There are carboxyl, amide, hydroxyl, carbonyl and other groups on the surface of CDs. Therefore, CDs can be used to detect heavy metal ions [16]. Besides, metal nanoclusters, another kind of metal based photoluminescent nano-materials, have been attracted continuous attention. Metal NCs can consist of several upon nearly 100 atoms with diameters not exceeding than 10 nm. Compared with other photoluminescent nano-materials, metal NCs have excellent photoluminescence properties, for instance, intense photoluminescence, high quantum yields, excellent photostability and large stokes shift, which is attributed to their size corresponding to the Fermi wavelength of electrons [7,17–19]. Therefore, the metal NCs already have been wide-ranging applied in various fields, including photoelectric device, bioimaging, chemical sensing and ions detection [20–24].

For the moment, a lot of photoluminescent materials for heavy metal ions detection have been reported, for example Kailasa et al. used water-soluble carbon dots and trypsin-modified gold nanoclusters as photoluminescent probes to detect Hg2+ [25,26]. These methods are to develop the change of emission peak intensity under single excitation wavelength (the intensity of fluorescence probe increases or decreases with the addition of metal ions). This method is susceptible to errors resulted in changes in probe concentration in complex samples and matrix effects [18,27]. To solve these problems, the dual-emission photoluminescent sensors have been developed to eliminate these side effects and provide more accurate results for metal ions detection. To date, some dual emission photoluminescent nano-composites have been designed and demonstrated using two different photoluminescent monomers with the same excitation wavelength. For example, Yan et al. used CDs and AuNCs to synthesize a novel photoluminescent sensor with dual-emission for detection of Hg2+ [7]. Although the dual-emission sensor exhibits excellent selectivity for detection of Hg2+, however, its unable to commercial production and high cost have hindered its practical application. Compared with the of Au, copper have the advantages of high performance-to-price ratio and rich mineral resources [28]. To our knowledge, there is little research reports concerning dual-emission CDs-CuNCs nano-composites are composed of CDs and CuNCs for colorimetric detection of Hg2+.

In this work, a novel dual-emission CDs-CuNCs nano-composites are constructed by the electrostatic assembly between CDs and CuNCs, the dual-emission CDs-CuNCs nano-composites exhibit supersensitive and selective toward Hg2+. Negatively charged CDs is prepared by one-step hydrothermal carbonization method using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as carbon source. Meanwhile, the CuNCs were prepared using Cu(NO3)2 and l-penicillamine as copper source and stabilizer. CuNCs with strong positive charge and CDs with negative charge formed CDs-CuNCs nano-composites by electrostatic assembly (as shown in Fig. S1 and Scheme 1 ). The resulting CDs-CuNCs nano-composites show two different emission wavelengths under the same excitation wavelength. The emission from CDs is used as reference signal, while emission from CuNCs is used as report signal. In the existence of Hg2+, the red photoluminescence of CuNCs is quenched, the blue photoluminescence of the CDs is stable. The PL color of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites gradually transformed from pink to blue along with the rise of Hg2+ concentrations. The CDs-CuNCs nano-composites show a low LOD (0.31 nM) in aqueous solution. In addition, a portable test paper for detection of Hg2+ is developed, which increase the practical application of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites from different aspects, for example, food security, environmental monitoring and biomedical detection.

Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2) was supplied by Shandong Yinuo Co. Ltd. Bovine serum albumin, l-penicillamine and mercury chloride (HgCl2) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial company of Shanghai. Distilled water was made by our laboratory. All reagents were of analytical grade and were used directly for experiments without any purification.

In this experiment, CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal way. Firstly, Dissolve 0.4 g BSA with 20 ml distilled water for ultrasonic treatment. Then the above solution was added into the 50 ml PTFE lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8.5 h. The obtained brown solution was naturally cooled, then large particles and impurities were separated by a 0.22 μM ion filter. The resulting CDs solution (15.4 mg mL−1) stored in a 4 °C refrigerator.

Dissolve 0.012 g l-penicillamine with 3 ml distilled water for ultrasonic treatment. Then 0.0036 g Cu(NO3)2 was dissolved in 100 μL distilled water, and putted in the above solution drop by drop under stirring for 5 min. Finally, 1.9 ml distilled water was added into the above mixture solution, and then stirred for 2 h to obtain the milky white solution. The prepared CuNCs (3.9 mg mL−1) was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the dual-emission sensor of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were designed according to the following steps. First, 0.25 ml CDs (15.4 mg mL−1) was diluted with 1.75 ml distilled water to form a pale yellow solution (3.0 mg mL−1). Then the solution was stirred by magnetic force with 1 ml CuNCs (3.9 mg mL−1) for 10 min. Next, the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were pink under ultraviolet light and dialyzed to remove free single CDs. The PL spectrum properties of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were researched, and it exhibited two strong emission peaks of 430 and 647 nm, when the excitation wavelength was 345 nm. The dual-emission CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were successfully prepared by the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ detection experiment using CDs-CuNCs nano-composites as probe
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensitivity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites toward different concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 1 × 103, 1 × 104, 3 × 104, 4 × 104 nM) and putted into the quartz cuvette. Then the PL spectrum of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were collected in the range of 355–800 nm, in which two emission peaks were obtained at 430 and 647 nm, when excitation wavelength was 345 nm. The PL stability and relative PL ratio intensity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were tested (slit width of 5 nm) [29]. To investigate the selectivity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites to other interfering metal ions (Ca2+, Na+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Cr6+, K+, Co2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cr3+). The CDs-CuNCs nano-composites had high selectivity for Hg2+, compared with other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PL test paper
ce:section.ce:para: A visible photoluminescent test paper for detection of Hg2+ was prepared with fiber filter paper. Immerse the test paper in dispersion CDs-CuNCs nano-composites solution for 15 min, took it out and dried it for use. Then 50 μL Hg2+ solution with various concentrations (0, 5, 10, 50, 100, 500, 3 × 104, 4 × 104 nM) were dropped into the test paper. The PL color of test paper could be observed intuitively with 365 nm UV light.

The microstructure of CDs, CuNCs and CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were studied through transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI TECNAI F20). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was collected by means of a AXISULTRADLD (Shimadze, Japan) spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra was collected through Cary 5000 UV–vis–NIR spectro photometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was observed by 50 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo scientific Nicolet). Photoluminescent excitation and emission spectra were conducted by PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the CDs, CuNCs and CDs-CuNCs nano-composites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and sizes of CDs, CuNCs and CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were studied by TEM. From Fig. 1 (a), TEM images showed that the sample was nearly spherical structure with diameter was about 2.25 nm, which displayed good monodispersity. In addition, the diameter of CuNCs was about 2.03 nm with good monodispersity (Fig. 1(b)). Fig. 1(c) was the TEM images of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites, which also confirmed that CDs and CuNCs were assembled as average size of the nano-composites was 7.09 nm by electrostatic assembly. EDS spectra also proved that CDs-CuNCs nano-composites could be successfully synthesized (Fig. 1(d)) [30].

To investigate the composition of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites, XPS test was characterized. It could be found that the samples contained C, N, O, S and Cu in XPS spectra (Fig. 2 (a)). For the high-resolution C 1s region in Fig. 2(b), three separated peaks at 286.3 (CN/CO), 284.5 (CC) and 288.6 eV (CO) from the CDs were observed [31]. From Fig. 2(c), the high-resolution N 1s region, the three peaks at 398.5, 400.0 and 402.7 eV, corresponded to C–N–C, N-(C3) and N–H, respectively [32]. The high-resolution O 1s region was divided into two peaks, which was CO/C–OH/CO (536.5 eV) and CO (531.6 eV) (Fig. 2(d)) [33]. For S 2p, the two peaks of S 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 were corresponded to 163.85 and 162.5 eV (Fig. 2(e)) [34–36]. The results indicated that the surface of the prepared CDs was rich in functional groups such as –OH, –NH2, –COOH, so that it had good water solubility and reactivity. It could be seen that two strong peaks at 955.7 and 936.0 eV belong to Cu 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 in Fig. 2(f), respectively [37,38], characteristic peaks of Cu(0). There were no peaks belong to Cu2+ at 942 eV, which meant CuNCs did not contain Cu2+. It could be proved that CuNCs were synthesized successfully.

The formation of CDs, CuNCs and CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were carried out through FT-IR spectroscopy measurements. The bending vibrations of O–H and stretching vibrations of C–H were corresponded to 3440 and 1400 cm−1, respectively, indicated that –OH and –CH exist on the superficies of CDs (Fig. S2(a)) [39]. The characteristic vibrations of 1636 cm−1 belonged to CO, and the peak of C–N stretching was occurred at 1348 cm−1, which indicated that there were carboxyl groups and acylamines surface of CDs. The existence of peaks for the stretching mode of COO− (1391 and 1495 cm−1), the characteristic peaks of N–H stretching (3448 cm−1) and N–H bending (1629 cm−1) of NH2 could be observed in Fig. S2(b), which also indicated that the surface of CuNCs contains carboxyl group (COO−) and amino-group (NH2) [40–42]. The vibration peak of –CONH could be observed at 1651 cm−1, which indicated that CuNCs had been combined with CDs (Fig. S2(c)) [43]. This result confirmed the successful synthesis of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The selective response of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites to Hg2+ due to the position difference of characteristic peaks of CDs and CuNCs (Fig. 5 (a)). It showed that the red photoluminescence from CuNCs at 647 nm slowly decreased and the blue photoluminescence from CDs at 430 nm remained basically unchanged, with the increase of Hg2+ concentration. In other words, When Hg2+ was dropped into the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites solution, the red photoluminescence of CuNCs was quenched, but the blue photoluminescence of CDs was still stable. This reason may be because the strong affinity of 5d10 and 3d10 between Hg2+ and CuNCs. This strong affinity made CuNCs combine with Hg2+, which made it lose its photoluminescent performance (Fig. S3) [22,24,44,45]. The linear relationship between the PL signals ratio of the two emission peaks (I430/I647) and Hg2+ concentration was established (Fig. 5(b)). After calculation, the linear regression equation of the PL signals and Hg2+ concentration (0–4 × 104 nM) was y = 0.14x+1.041, R2 = 0.9942 (the inset of Fig. 5(b)). It was noteworthy that the low LOD of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites was calculated to be 0.31 nM (LOD = 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution and K is the slope of the simple equation, the determination times of blank solution are 3) [46–48]. Table S1 showed the LOD of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites, compared with related different nano-materials toward Hg2+. The CDs-CuNCs nano-composites exhibited better advantages than reported methods previously. Table S2 showed the analytical performance of the double emission photoluminescent detection method based on the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites and the method reported in the literature, indicated that the present method has better sensitivity than that of other methods for the detection of Hg2+. The continuous change of photoluminescent color was attributed to the change of PL intensity of two emission peaks. A CIE diagram showed that the PL color of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites changed with the influence of different concentrations of Hg2+ in Fig. 5(c). When the concentration of Hg2+ was slowly added, the PL color gradually changed from pink to blue. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the obtained optical photograph under the ultraviolet lamp also confirmed this result. In addition, this colorimetric detection was more recognizable than the monochromatic detection of CuNCs. The CDs-CuNCs nano-composites had unique PL emission characteristics, which could be further applied to visual PL and biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, high stability and specificity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites to Hg2+ after added EDTA was studied (Fig. S4). The results showed that the PL of CDs-CuNCs nano-composites could be restored to 85% of the initial value and the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites had excellent repeatability. The PL stability of nano-composites was one of the important factors to evaluate their applications [48]. In Fig. S5(a), the stability of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites was systematically investigated for various storage time, the PL intensity ratio had no significantly changed, implied the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were stable. Fig. S5(b) presented the PL intensities of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites at pH 4–8. It showed that the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites had a stable PL intensity in the pH range of 4–8.

To further research the specificity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites for Hg2+, the PL properties of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were carefully examined when other environmentally relevant metal ions exist, for instance, Ca2+, Na+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Cr6+, K+, Co2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Cr3+, as shown in Fig. 6 . The above results were derived from the original data spectrogram (Fig. S6). There was almost no response under the interference of other metal ions at concentration of 50 nM, while the PL intensity of CuNCs dramatically quenched by Hg2+(10 nM). The results indicated that the selectivity of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites for Hg2+ was better than other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection for Hg2+ using the PL test paper
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reponse the requirements of economic and rapid determination for Hg2+, a simple sensitive and low-cost test paper was developed. When exposed to a certain concentration of Hg2+, the test paper quickly changed from pink to blue with a 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7 ). This may be due to test paper based on nano-composites still maintain the same dispersibility as in aqueous solution (Fig. S7). And Fig. S8 showed the selective response of the test paper to Hg2+ and the linear relationship between the PL intensity ratio of the two emission peaks (I430/I647) and Hg2+ concentration. Compared with the reported test paper [49], This test paper had better sensitivity, specificity, and a lower LOD (0.78 nM). The above results showed that the test paper was low cost, simple, sensitive and a potential prospect in the detection of Hg2+.

In an effort to verify CDs-CuNCs nano-composites response to the different concentrations of Hg2+ (0, 0.5, 5, 50 nM) in real samples, lake water (supported by Nanhu Park in Changchun, China) and tap water were used as experimental objects. The real samples were filtered by 0.22 μM microfilter to remove large size particles and impurities. The recovery rates of the lake water and tap water were 104.2–106% and 108–108.8%, respectively (Table S3). The original data of Table S3 were from Fig. S9. Thus, the results suggested that the nano-composites had good precision and practical application in real samples for Hg2+ detection.

In short, a dual-emission CDs-CuNCs nano-composites were synthetised by simple electrostatic assembly between the CDs and CuNCs for sensitive detection of Hg2+. The CDs-CuNCs nano-composites demonstrated strong emission at 430 nm of blue photoluminescence (CDs) and 647 nm of red photoluminescence (CuNCs), respectively. The red emission from CuNCs exhibited good selectivity for Hg2+, while the blue emission from CDs remained stable. With the added of Hg2+ concentration, the PL color of the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites gradually changed from pink to blue. It was as a promising PL nano-composites toward Hg2+ detection showed high selectivity, good sensitivity, ultra-low LOD (0.31 nM). In addition, we also proved that the CDs-CuNCs nano-composites could be used in the preparation of test paper and had potential application foreground in the aspects of environmental analysis, food safety detection, biological detection and medical diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We were very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (No. 51603018), the Project of Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission, China (No. 2019C050-10), Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, Jilin University, China (sklssm2021028).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2021.03.143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bonding of ppb levels of methyl mercury to reduced sulfur groups in soil organic matter
